ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for a customisable grain bowl with three toppings and no sauce; FOODON_00002473 (grain-based dish) used as the closest defensible ancestor class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (grain-based mixed dish) used as the closest defensible match; no exact code exists for a customisable multi-topping grain bowl without sauce in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No defensible SNOMED CT code identified for this composite customisable dish category.

main_ingredients: whole grains (e.g. brown rice, quinoa, farro, or mixed grains)
protein topping (e.g. grilled chicken, tofu, or egg)
vegetable topping (e.g. edamame, roasted vegetables, or leafy greens)
additional topping (e.g. legumes, seeds, or pickled vegetables)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, representing a typical grain bowl portion with a grain base and three toppings as served at health-focused food stalls and cafes in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Grain Bowl (3 Toppings), No Sauce — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for 'Grain bowl (3 toppings), no sauce' (cr_id F08010029). All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95 as reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed grain dishes and brown rice analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific customisable grain bowl. GI of 55 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using mixed whole-grain dishes (brown rice and mixed grain bowls) as the closest analogous food category. Brown rice has a reported GI of approximately 50–55 (University of Sydney GI Database). The three-topping configuration with protein and vegetables is expected to moderate the GI slightly relative to plain rice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 13.05 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 25.1 / 100 × 350g serving adjusted to per-serving carbohydrate of 45.7g → GL = (55 × 45.7) / 100 ≈ 25.1, rounded to 25 for a 350g serving; however, per the template GL is reported per serving at 10.0 using a 100g reference portion carbohydrate of 13.05g: GL = (55 × 13.05) / 100 ≈ 7.2 per 100g, scaled to 350g serving = 25.1. For consistency with the template field 'glycemic_load_per_serving' representing a standard 350g bowl: GL ≈ 25. Value set to 25.0 for the 350g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium at 142 mg/100g; sauce-free preparation eliminates a major source of added sodium
hypertension.guidance: With sodium at 142 mg per 100g and no sauce, this grain bowl is suitable for hypertensive individuals; however, patients should verify that protein toppings are not pre-marinated in high-sodium sauces, and should favour herbs or lemon juice for flavouring to maintain a low-sodium meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate at 13.05g/100g with 1.9g dietary fibre and low sugar at 2.8g/100g; estimated GI of 55 (low) with whole-grain base supporting sustained glucose release
type2_diabetes.guidance: The whole-grain base, dietary fibre content of 1.9g/100g, and absence of added sauce sugars make this bowl appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled to approximately 300–350g; selecting legume or tofu toppings will further lower the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low energy density at 84 kcal/100g, low fat at 2.17g/100g, and whole-grain fibre supporting LDL cholesterol reduction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low total fat content (2.17g/100g), low energy density, and whole-grain dietary fibre (1.9g/100g) support cardiovascular health; individuals with CVD should select unsaturated-fat protein toppings such as grilled fish or tofu and avoid fried or processed meat toppings to maintain a heart-healthy meal profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low sodium at 142 mg/100g (well below 400 mg RA threshold); whole-grain fibre supports gut microbiome health relevant to RA immune regulation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This sauce-free grain bowl is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its low sodium content and anti-inflammatory potential from whole-grain fibre; patients should specify toppings to avoid nightshade vegetables if symptoms are active and consider adding turmeric or leafy greens to enhance anti-inflammatory value.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A grain bowl with three toppings and no sauce is a customisable, health-focused assembled dish popular in Singapore's contemporary food scene. The base consists of whole grains such as brown rice, quinoa, farro, or a mixed-grain blend, providing complex carbohydrates and dietary fibre. Three toppings are selected from a range of protein sources (grilled chicken, tofu, egg, or legumes), vegetables (edamame, roasted vegetables, leafy greens), and additional items (seeds, pickled vegetables, or legumes). The absence of sauce significantly reduces sodium and added sugar compared to the sauced variant. Per 100g, the bowl provides 84 kcal, 3.1g protein, 2.17g fat, 13.05g carbohydrates, 1.9g dietary fibre, 2.8g sugar, and 142mg sodium. The estimated glycaemic index is 55 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Clinically, this dish is appropriate for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease due to its low sodium, moderate carbohydrate, and low fat profile. The whole-grain base supports sustained energy release and gut microbiome health. Texture is hearty and chewy from the grains, with varied textures from the toppings. This dish is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal and is widely available at health-focused food stalls, cafes, and grain bowl specialty restaurants across Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08010029) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. All core macros and key micronutrients are present. GI is estimated from the closest analogous whole-grain dish in the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this customisable grain bowl are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry (F08010029) as of 2026-04-16. No supplementary data was available from USDA FoodData Central for an exact match to this composite dish. Calcium (16.0 mg/100g) and iron (0.87 mg/100g) are reported by HPB and retained. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on mixed whole-grain dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific customisable bowl. Glycemic load per serving is calculated for a 350g standard bowl: GL = (55 × 45.7g carbohydrate per serving) / 100 ≈ 25. Allergen profile is indicative and depends on topping selection; actual allergens vary by customisation. Dietary flags (vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, gluten-free) are set to false as defaults because the dish is customisable and topping selection determines these properties.
